## BUSINESS

### Overview

Crestline Software, Inc. is an enterprise SaaS company providing an AI-driven supply chain optimization platform to Fortune 1000 manufacturers and retailers. Our platform, Crestline Nexus, is powered by the proprietary SupplyMind AI engine, which combines machine learning, advanced statistical modeling, and real-time data integration to deliver predictive and prescriptive supply chain intelligence.

### Our Products

**Crestline Nexus Platform.** A unified SaaS platform for end-to-end supply chain optimization, with real-time data ingestion from ERP, WMS, TMS, and IoT systems, pre-built connectors for over 100 enterprise data sources, and a multi-tenant, cloud-native architecture.

**SupplyMind AI Engine.** Proprietary machine learning models for demand forecasting, inventory optimization, and logistics routing, protected by our patent portfolio.

### Market Opportunity

We estimate the total addressable market for AI-driven supply chain software to be approximately $28 billion by 2028, with a serviceable addressable market of approximately $9 billion focused on Fortune 1000 manufacturers and retailers. We believe current penetration is less than 3% of the serviceable addressable market.

### Customers

As of December 31, 2024, we served 487 customers across manufacturing, retail, logistics, consumer packaged goods, and other industries. Our top 10 customers represented approximately 31.7% of fiscal year 2024 revenue, and our largest customer represented approximately 8.2% of fiscal year 2024 revenue. No single customer accounted for more than 10% of total revenue in fiscal year 2024 or 2023. Our dollar-based net retention rate was 132% for fiscal year 2024.

### Intellectual Property

As of December 31, 2024, our intellectual property portfolio consisted of 47 issued U.S. patents, 12 pending U.S. patent applications, and 8 issued foreign patents. Our material trademarks include "Crestline," "Crestline Nexus," and "SupplyMind," each registered with the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. We achieved SOC 2 Type II certification in August 2024. We are subject to the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) through our UK subsidiary and the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA).

### Employees

As of December 31, 2024, we had 1,247 employees.

### Properties

We lease our principal executive offices in Austin, Texas (our headquarters, expiring August 2029, with annual base rent of approximately $3.8 million), and additional offices in San Francisco, California (expiring March 2027, annual base rent approximately $2.1 million); London, United Kingdom (expiring January 2028, annual base rent approximately £680,000); and Bangalore, India (expiring June 2026, annual base rent approximately INR 42,000 thousand).

### Legal Proceedings

On September 12, 2024, Optivance, Inc. filed a patent infringement lawsuit against us in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Texas (Case No. 6:24-cv-00831), alleging that certain features of our SupplyMind platform infringe U.S. Patent No. 11,284,107, relating to "machine-learning-based inventory forecasting." We believe the claims are without merit and intend to vigorously defend against them. The lawsuit is in the early stages, and no trial date has been set. We have not recorded a loss contingency for this matter because management believes it is not probable that a loss has been incurred. Based on the advice of outside patent counsel, we estimate the potential range of loss, in the event of an adverse judgment, to be between $5.0 million and $20.0 million, although an unfavorable outcome is considered to have a low probability. An adverse judgment in excess of $5.0 million that remains undischarged for 60 days could constitute an event of default under our term loan credit agreement.

## CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PARTY TRANSACTIONS

The agreement provides for annual subscription fees of $1.2 million over a three-year term, for a total contract value of $3.6 million. The transaction was reviewed and approved by our Audit Committee, with Ms. Venkatesh recused from the deliberation and vote. The Audit Committee concluded that the terms were comparable to those offered to similarly situated customers. Revenue recognized from Vantara was $1.2 million during fiscal year 2024 and $0.9 million during fiscal year 2023. Accounts receivable from Vantara as of December 31, 2024 and 2023 were $0.3 million and $0.25 million, respectively.

**Preferred Stock Financings.** Graymont Ventures participated in our Series A (October 2018), Series B (April 2020), and Series D (June 2023) financing rounds, and Ridgeline Growth Partners participated in our Series C (November 2021) and Series D (June 2023) financing rounds, in each case on terms negotiated at arm's length and approved by our Board of Directors.

**Investor Rights Agreement.** In June 2023, we entered into an Investor Rights Agreement, Voting Agreement, and Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement with certain preferred stockholders in connection with the Series D financing. The Voting Agreement and Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement terminate upon the closing of this offering. The Investor Rights Agreement provides registration rights that survive this offering. See "Description of Capital Stock—Registration Rights."

**Prior Employment of General Counsel.** Sandra Y. Chen, our General Counsel and Corporate Secretary, was previously employed as an associate at Thorncroft & Wexler LLP, our outside counsel. Ms. Chen has no ongoing financial interest in the firm.

**Policies and Procedures.** We have adopted a related party transaction policy that requires the review and approval or ratification of transactions between us and related parties by our Audit Committee.

## RECENT SALES OF UNREGISTERED SECURITIES

Since our incorporation on March 14, 2017, we have issued the following unregistered securities:

**Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock.** We issued an aggregate of 35,416,667 shares of redeemable convertible preferred stock in four series: 8,000,000 shares of Series A at $1.50 per share in October 2018 (aggregate $12.0 million); 9,500,000 shares of Series B at $4.00 per share in April 2020 (aggregate $38.0 million); 7,916,667 shares of Series C at $12.00 per share in November 2021 (aggregate $95.0 million); and 10,000,000 shares of Series D at $24.00 per share in June 2023 (aggregate $240.0 million). These shares were issued in reliance on the exemption from registration afforded by Section 4(a)(2) of the Securities Act and/or Rule 506 of Regulation D promulgated thereunder. **2024 Equity Incentive Plan and 2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan.** Our 2024 Equity Incentive Plan (authorizing 18,500,000 shares) and 2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan (reserving 2,500,000 shares) were adopted by our Board of Directors and approved by our stockholders on January 15, 2024. The 2024 Plan is the successor to our 2017 Stock Option Plan, and all outstanding awards under the 2017 Plan were rolled into the 2024 Plan. The ESPP will become effective upon the closing of this offering.

## DESCRIPTION OF REGISTRANT'S SECURITIES TO BE REGISTERED

Except as described in this prospectus, there have been no material changes in our affairs since December 31, 2024.

## Item 15. Recent Sales of Unregistered Securities

## Item 16. Exhibits and Financial Statement Schedules

Exhibits to this registration statement include our amended and restated certificate of incorporation, amended and restated bylaws, the term loan credit agreement with Kestridge West Credit Partners, the Investor Rights Agreement, the Voting Agreement, the Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement, the 2024 Equity Incentive Plan, the 2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan, the form of underwriting agreement, the form of lock-up agreement, and the consent of our independent registered public accounting firm, Archer Pryce LLP.

The report of Archer Pryce LLP, which has served as our auditor since 2022, contains an unqualified opinion and identifies two critical audit matters: (i) revenue recognition for arrangements with multiple performance obligations and (ii) the valuation of common stock underlying stock-based compensation awards.

{"content": "# MEMORANDUM — PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL

**TO:** Board of Directors and Management, Crestline Software, Inc.; Sarah K. Norden, Esq., Thorncroft & Wexler LLP (Issuer's Counsel)
**FROM:** [IPO Working Group — Securities Counsel]
**DATE:** [●], 2025
**RE:** Inconsistencies, Disclosure Gaps, and Risks Identified in the Source Record for the Proposed Form S-1 Registration Statement — Crestline Software, Inc. (Proposed Nasdaq: CRST)

## PURPOSE AND SCOPE

This memorandum flags inconsistencies, internal arithmetic errors, cross-document conflicts, and disclosure risks identified during a review of the document set assembled in connection with Crestline Software, Inc.'s proposed initial public offering of Class A common stock. The documents reviewed are: (1) the audited consolidated financial statements for fiscal years 2023–2024 (Archer Pryce LLP); (2) the CFO IPO readiness memorandum dated February 14, 2025; (3) the amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws; (4) the term loan summary (Kestridge West Credit Partners); (5) the underwriting term sheet and lock-up; (6) the director and officer questionnaire responses (Thorncroft & Wexler LLP); (7) the equity plan summary and grants workbook; and (8) the draft IPO roadshow presentation.

Each finding below states the issue, the specific evidence (with exact figures and the document in which it appears), the consequence for the registration statement or the offering, and a recommended action. Findings are grouped by category and numbered consecutively. A prioritized action list appears at the end. This memorandum is intended to be read together with the draft Form S-1 and does not itself constitute legal advice on any single item; several items require confirmation against primary records (stock ledger, credit agreement, equity plan records, and 409A valuation reports) before the registration statement is filed.

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The record supports filing a Form S-1, but it contains a substantial number of inconsistencies that must be reconciled before filing. The most serious are:

- **The headline non-GAAP metric is arithmetically wrong.** Adjusted EBITDA is stated as $22.8 million in the CFO memo and the roadshow, but the stated add-backs sum to $20.5 million. The FY2023 reconciliation line items in the CFO memo also do not match the audited financial statements. Presenting an incorrect non-GAAP reconciliation risks a Regulation G / Item 10(e) violation and an SEC comment letter.
- **The term loan is described inconsistently across every document that touches it** — closing date (December 2022 vs. June 2023), draw amounts, amortization schedule, and even the lender's name ("Kestridge West Credit Partners" vs. "Silicon Valley Credit Partners").
- **The cash flow statement does not tie to the balance sheet** (beginning cash for FY2024 is $78.4 million but the prior year-end balance sheet shows $68.5 million), and FY2023 short-term investment purchases of $215 million cannot reconcile to the $38.5 million balance.
- **The co-founder's legal name is inconsistent** ("Katerina Litvak" in the charter, bylaws, and underwriting term sheet vs. "Katerina Litvanova" in the CFO memo and D&O questionnaires), and it is unclear whether she is a director (charter says 7 directors including her; the D&O questionnaire says 6 directors excluding her).
- **The equity plan records do not reconcile to the audited financial statements** (option and RSU grants, exercises, forfeitures, and the 409A valuation history all conflict), and the equity plan ledger contains wrong director names and wrong officer titles.
- **A related-party customer (Vantara Logistics) is presented as an ordinary customer in the roadshow and CFO memo** without the required Item 404 disclosure, and the roadshow repeatedly mischaracterizes professional services as "high-margin" when the audited statements show a negative professional services gross margin.

Each of these, and the additional findings below, should be resolved and reflected consistently in the S-1, the roadshow, and all offering documents.

---

## I. FINANCIAL STATEMENT AND NON-GAAP INCONSISTENCIES

### Finding 1 — Adjusted EBITDA is arithmetically incorrect ($22.8M stated; $20.5M correct)

**Issue.** The Adjusted EBITDA figure of $22.8 million for FY2024 does not equal the sum of the reconciliation line items as presented.

**Evidence.** The CFO readiness memorandum (Section 5.2) and the roadshow (Slides 3, 23, 34, and 35) each present the following bridge: Net loss $(24.8M) + D&A $8.7M + Stock-based compensation $38.2M + Interest expense $3.1M − Interest income $6.2M + Income tax provision $0.3M + Restructuring charges $1.2M. The sum of these items is $20.5 million, not $22.8 million. The roadshow's own drafting notes acknowledge this error (Slide 23 notes: "Performing the arithmetic: −$24.8 + $8.7 + $38.2 + (−$3.1) + $0.3 + $1.2 = $20.5 million, NOT $22.8 million. The $2.3M discrepancy is unexplained."). The stated Adjusted EBITDA margin of 12.2% is likewise incorrect; $20.5M / $187.3M = 10.9%.

**Consequence.** The individual FY2024 add-backs match the audited financial statements, so the error is in the total only. Nonetheless, presenting an incorrect non-GAAP reconciliation in the S-1 would violate Regulation G and Item 10(e) of Regulation S-K and would likely draw an SEC comment letter. The same $22.8M figure is repeated in the roadshow, creating a consistency problem if the S-1 uses the correct $20.5M.

**Recommendation.** Use $20.5 million (10.9% margin) as Adjusted EBITDA for FY2024 in the S-1 and all offering materials. Correct the roadshow. Confirm whether any additional add-back (e.g., a $2.3M item omitted from the bridge) was intended; if so, disclose it explicitly and reconcile.

### Finding 2 — FY2023 Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation line items do not match the audited financial statements

**Issue.** Every FY2023 line item in the CFO memo's Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation differs from the audited financial statements.

**Evidence.** CFO memo Section 5.2 (FY2023 column) vs. audited financial statements:

| Line item | CFO memo (FY2023) | Audited (FY2023) |
|---|---|---|
| Depreciation & amortization | $6.9M | $7.1M (cash flow statement) |
| Stock-based compensation | $29.1M | $26.4M (Note 12(d); cash flow) |
| Interest expense | $2.8M | $3.1M (statements of operations) |
| Interest income | $4.6M | $5.8M (statements of operations) |
| Income tax provision | $0.2M | $0.1M (statements of operations) |
| Restructuring charges | $0.6M | $0 (Note 9 shows no FY2023 restructuring) |

The CFO memo's FY2023 line items sum (with net loss $27.4M) to $7.6M. Using the audited figures, FY2023 Adjusted EBITDA is approximately $3.5M, not $7.6M.

**Consequence.** The S-1's non-GAAP reconciliation must use the audited figures. The $7.6M figure overstates FY2023 Adjusted EBITDA by approximately $4.1M and materially overstates the year-over-year improvement narrative.

**Recommendation.** Rebuild the FY2023 (and FY2024) Adjusted EBITDA reconciliations directly from the audited financial statements. Use $3.5M for FY2023 (subject to confirmation of the restructuring add-back, which is $0 in the audited statements).

### Finding 3 — Restructuring description is internally inconsistent (headcount and timing)

**Issue.** The workforce reduction is described differently in the CFO memo and the audited financial statements.

**Evidence.** Audited financial statements Note 9: "During the third quarter of 2024, the Company implemented a workforce reduction affecting approximately 46 employees. The Company recorded restructuring charges of $1.2 million." CFO memo Section 5.3: "The restructuring charges of $1.2 million in FY 2024 relate to a workforce reduction of approximately 30 positions in our professional services organization in the second quarter of 2024."

**Consequence.** The S-1 MD&A and the financial statement notes must agree. The headcount (46 vs. 30) and the quarter (Q3 vs. Q2) conflict.

**Recommendation.** Use the audited figures (approximately 46 employees, Q3 2024) in the S-1. Correct the CFO memo and roadshow.

### Finding 4 — Subscription revenue and growth rate for FY2023 are misstated in the CFO memo

**Issue.** The CFO memo's FY2023 subscription revenue and the resulting growth rate do not match the audited financial statements.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 3: subscription revenue $116.4M (FY2023) and $168.6M (FY2024); professional services $12.5M (FY2023) and $18.7M (FY2024). CFO memo Section 4.1: subscription $113.4M (FY2023) and $168.6M (FY2024), with stated growth of 48.7%; professional services $15.5M (FY2023) and $18.7M (FY2024), with stated growth of 20.6%. The CFO memo's FY2023 split ($113.4M + $15.5M = $128.9M) sums to the correct total but misallocates $3.0M from subscription to professional services.

**Consequence.** The correct subscription growth rate is ($168.6M − $116.4M) / $116.4M = 44.8%, not 48.7%. The correct professional services growth rate is ($18.7M − $12.5M) / $12.5M = 49.6%, not 20.6%. The S-1 must use the audited split.

**Recommendation.** Use the audited figures ($116.4M subscription, $12.5M professional services for FY2023) in the S-1 MD&A and key operating metrics. Correct the CFO memo and roadshow.

### Finding 5 — Professional services are mischaracterized as "high-margin" (actual margin is negative)

**Issue.** The roadshow repeatedly describes professional services as "high-margin and accretive" and projects 15–20% gross margins, but the audited financial statements show a negative professional services gross margin.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 4: professional services revenue $18.7M and professional services cost of revenue $18.8M for FY2024, a gross margin of approximately (0.5)%. Roadshow Slides 12, 17, 21, and 27 describe professional services as "high-margin, accretive" and project 15–20% margins. The roadshow's own notes flag this (Slide 12 notes; Slide 21 notes: "The audited financials for FY 2024 show professional services revenue of $18.7M and professional services COGS of $18.8M, yielding a gross margin of approximately −0.5% (negative).").

**Consequence.** Characterizing a margin-negative business as "high-margin" is misleading and would be inconsistent with the audited financial statements in the S-1. The S-1 MD&A must disclose the actual negative professional services gross margin and frame any 15–20% figure as a forward-looking target with appropriate cautionary language.

**Recommendation.** In the S-1, disclose the actual (0.5)% professional services gross margin. If a 15–20% target is used, label it as a forward-looking statement and reconcile to current results. Remove "high-margin" language from the roadshow.

### Finding 6 — Cash flow statement does not tie to the balance sheet (beginning cash)

**Issue.** The beginning cash balance in the FY2024 cash flow statement does not equal the ending cash balance on the December 31, 2023 balance sheet, and the two annual cash flow statements do not roll forward.

**Evidence.** Balance sheet: cash and cash equivalents $68,500 at December 31, 2023. Cash flow statement: "Cash and cash equivalents, beginning of year" $78,400 for FY2024 (which should equal the December 31, 2023 balance of $68,500). The FY2023 cash flow statement ends at $68,500, but the FY2024 statement begins at $78,400 — a $9,900 gap. The FY2024 statement is internally consistent ($78,400 + $16,300 net increase = $94,700 ending, matching the December 31, 2024 balance sheet), but it does not connect to the prior year.

**Consequence.** A cash flow statement that does not roll forward from the prior-year balance sheet is a basic financial statement tie-out error that the SEC staff will flag and that the auditor must address before the audit report is finalized.

**Recommendation.** Reconcile the FY2024 beginning cash balance to the December 31, 2023 balance sheet ($68,500). Identify the $9,900 discrepancy (likely an omitted cash flow item or an erroneous beginning balance) and correct the cash flow statement. Have Archer Pryce LLP re-issue the cash flow statement with a corrected roll-forward.

### Finding 7 — FY2023 short-term investment purchases do not reconcile to the balance sheet

**Issue.** The FY2023 cash flow statement reports $215.0 million of short-term investment purchases, which cannot reconcile to the short-term investments balance on the balance sheet.

**Evidence.** Cash flow statement FY2023: "Purchases of short-term investments" $(215,000) (i.e., $215.0 million) and "Maturities of short-term investments" $25,000. Net change in investments would be +$190.0 million, but the balance sheet shows short-term investments of only $38.5 million at December 31, 2023 (and $42.3 million at December 31, 2024). A $190.0 million increase is impossible given the $38.5 million ending balance (it would require a negative beginning balance). The FY2024 figures do reconcile ($28.8M purchases − $25.0M maturities = +$3.8M, matching $38.5M → $42.3M).

**Consequence.** The $215.0 million figure is almost certainly a typographical error (likely intended to be approximately $28.5 million, consistent with FY2024's $28.8 million). As stated, it makes the FY2023 investing section and the net cash figures internally inconsistent with the balance sheet.

**Recommendation.** Correct the FY2023 short-term investment purchases figure so that the investing section reconciles to the balance sheet. Confirm the correct figure with the Company's accounting records and have the auditor re-issue.

### Finding 8 — CFO memo condensed balance sheet misstates several line items vs. the audited balance sheet

**Issue.** The CFO memo's condensed balance sheet (Section 6.1) misstates accounts receivable, prepaid expenses, accounts payable, and accrued liabilities relative to the audited balance sheet, even though the subtotals are correct.

**Evidence.** CFO memo Section 6.1 vs. audited balance sheet (December 31, 2024):

| Line item | CFO memo | Audited |
|---|---|---|
| Accounts receivable, net | $28.2M | $29.8M |
| Prepaid expenses and other current assets | $13.2M | $11.6M |
| Accounts payable | $9.8M | $8.2M |
| Accrued liabilities | $14.7M | $12.1M |

The CFO memo appears to have absorbed the $4.2 million current operating lease liability into accounts payable and accrued liabilities (the current-asset and current-liability subtotals of $178.4M and $72.8M are correct, but the components are misstated).

**Consequence.** Any S-1 disclosure sourced from the CFO memo (rather than the audited statements) would carry forward these misstatements. The S-1 must use the audited balance sheet line items.

**Recommendation.** Source all balance sheet disclosures in the S-1 directly from the audited financial statements. Correct the CFO memo.

### Finding 9 — Weighted-average share count equals year-end share count for both years

**Issue.** The weighted-average shares used in the EPS computation equal the year-end share count for both fiscal years, even though shares were issued during each year.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 16 and statements of operations: weighted-average shares basic and diluted of 52,000,000 (FY2024) and 50,500,000 (FY2023). The equity rollforward (Section 4) shows common shares increasing from 50,500,000 to 52,000,000 during FY2024 (via 800,000 option exercises and 700,000 RSU vestings) and from 49,200,000 to 50,500,000 during FY2023. A weighted-average denominator should fall between the beginning and ending counts when shares are issued during the year; using the year-end count as the weighted average is unusual.

**Consequence.** If the weighted-average denominator is overstated, the net loss per share is understated in magnitude (less negative). This is a potential EPS computation error that the SEC staff and the auditor should confirm.

**Recommendation.** Confirm with Archer Pryce LLP that the weighted-average share counts are correctly computed (including the timing of option exercises and RSU vestings). If the year-end counts were used in error, recompute EPS and re-issue the affected statements.

---

## II. TERM LOAN AND DEBT INCONSISTENCIES

### Finding 10 — Term loan closing date is inconsistent (December 2022 vs. June 2023)

**Issue.** The date the Company entered into the term loan facility is stated differently in the audited financial statements and the term loan summary.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 8: "In June 2023, the Company entered into a term loan facility with Kestridge West Credit Partners." Term loan summary Section 1: "Closing Date of Credit Agreement: December 15, 2022," and Section 10: "Initial Draw Conditions (satisfied at closing on December 15, 2022)."

**Consequence.** The S-1 "Description of Indebtedness" and the financial statement notes must state a single, correct closing date. The discrepancy also affects the FY2022 and FY2023 cash flow presentation.

**Recommendation.** Confirm the correct closing date against the credit agreement. If the credit agreement was executed December 15, 2022, correct Note 8; if June 2023, correct the term loan summary. Reconcile the cash flow statement accordingly.

### Finding 11 — Term loan draw, repayment, and balance do not reconcile across the financial statements

**Issue.** The cash flow statement's term loan activity does not reconcile to the balance sheet and Note 8, and the term loan summary's draw history conflicts with both.

**Evidence.** (a) Balance sheet and Note 8: term loan outstanding $40.0 million at both December 31, 2023 and December 31, 2024. (b) Cash flow statement: "Proceeds from term loan borrowings" $15.0 million in FY2023 and "Repayment of term loan" $5.0 million in FY2024. If $15.0 million was borrowed in FY2023 and $5.0 million repaid in FY2024, the balance cannot remain $40.0 million at both year-ends (it would imply a $35.0 million balance at December 31, 2024, or require unreported FY2024 borrowings). (c) Term loan summary Section 1: "The initial draw of $40,000,000 was made on the closing date," with no subsequent draws (the $10.0 million undrawn commitment expired June 30, 2024). This conflicts with the $15.0 million of FY2023 borrowings in the cash flow statement.

**Consequence.** The term loan activity in the cash flow statement, the balance sheet, Note 8, and the term loan summary are mutually inconsistent. This is a material financial statement issue.

**Recommendation.** Reconcile the term loan rollforward (beginning balance + borrowings − repayments = ending balance) across all four sources and correct the inconsistent figures. Confirm the actual draw history against the lender's records.

### Finding 12 — Term loan amortization schedule is inconsistent

**Issue.** The future principal repayment schedule in Note 8 does not match the amortization described in the term loan summary.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 8 future principal repayments: 2025 $5.0M; 2026 $10.0M; 2027 $25.0M (total $40.0M). Term loan summary Section 3: amortization of 1.25% of the original drawn amount per quarter ($500,000 per quarter, or $2.0 million per year), with the remaining balance due as a bullet at maturity. A $2.0 million-per-year amortization would produce approximately $2.0M (2025), $2.0M (2026), and $36.0M (2027 bullet), not $5.0M / $10.0M / $25.0M.

**Consequence.** The S-1's contractual obligations table and "Description of Indebtedness" must state a single amortization schedule. The Note 8 schedule (escalating payments) is inconsistent with level quarterly amortization.

**Recommendation.** Confirm the correct amortization schedule against the credit agreement. Reconcile Note 8, the term loan summary, and the S-1 contractual obligations table.

### Finding 13 — Lender name is inconsistent ("Kestridge West" vs. "Silicon Valley" Credit Partners)

**Issue.** The roadshow refers to the lender by a different name than the audited financial statements, the CFO memo, and the term loan summary.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 8, CFO memo, and term loan summary: "Kestridge West Credit Partners" (the term loan summary abbreviates this "SVCP"). Roadshow Slides 20, 24, and 27 (and their notes): "Silicon Valley Credit Partners."

**Consequence.** The S-1 must use the lender's correct legal name. An incorrect lender name in the roadshow creates an inconsistency with the prospectus.

**Recommendation.** Confirm the lender's correct legal name from the credit agreement. Use it consistently in the S-1, the roadshow, and all offering documents. (Note: the abbreviation "SVCP" in the term loan summary appears to correspond to "Silicon Valley Credit Partners," suggesting possible confusion between two names; resolve which is correct.)

### Finding 14 — Use of proceeds and pro forma capitalization are inconsistent regarding debt repayment

**Issue.** The pro forma balance sheet assumes the term loan remains outstanding, while the use of proceeds contemplates debt repayment, and the proposed allocation differs across documents.

**Evidence.** (a) CFO memo Exhibit A "Pro Forma As Adjusted" shows the full $40.0 million term loan outstanding and the full $331.8 million of net proceeds added to cash (cash $426.5 million). CFO memo Section 3.3 likewise computes a $468.8 million pro forma cash position assuming no debt repayment. (b) CFO memo Section 3.2 allocates approximately 15% (~$49.8 million) to debt repayment (including up to $40.0 million of the term loan). (c) Roadshow Slide 27 allocates only ~5% (~$16.6 million) to term loan repayment. (d) Underwriting term sheet Section 4 states the Company "has not determined the specific allocation of net proceeds." These four positions are mutually inconsistent.

**Consequence.** The S-1's "Use of Proceeds" and the pro forma capitalization table must be consistent. If proceeds may be used to repay debt, the pro forma balance sheet should reflect that (or the S-1 should present the pro forma on a basis consistent with the stated use). The divergent percentage allocations (15% vs. 5% vs. undetermined) must be reconciled.

**Recommendation.** Decide on a single use-of-proceeds allocation. If the allocation is not determined, state that plainly in the S-1 (consistent with the term sheet) and do not present specific percentages. Ensure the pro forma capitalization table is consistent with the stated use. Note that repayment of debt with proceeds does not change net tangible book value (it reduces cash and liabilities equally), but it does change the pro forma cash and debt balances presented.

## III. CAPITALIZATION AND BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP

### Finding 15 — Board size is inconsistent (7 directors vs. 6 directors); unclear whether the co-founder/CTO is a director

**Issue.** The charter provides for a seven-member classified board including the co-founder/CTO, while the D&O questionnaire states the board has six members and that the co-founder/CTO is not a director.

**Evidence.** Charter Article V, Section 5.2: "The initial number of directors … shall be seven (7)." Section 5.3 assigns three classes totaling seven, including "Katerina Litvak" as a Class III director (term expiring 2028). D&O questionnaire Section 6: "The Board of Directors consists of six members … Note: Katerina Litvanova, Michael D. Hartwell, and Sandra Y. Chen are executive officers but do not serve on the Board." The roadshow is internally inconsistent: Slide 7 refers to "three of five non-employee directors" (implying six total), while Slide 30 states "3 of 7 total directors." The underwriting term sheet lock-up Schedule 1 lists five non-employee directors (consistent with a six-member board).

**Consequence.** The S-1 "Management" section, the classified-board disclosure, the lock-up party list, and the controlled-company analysis all depend on the correct board size. A classified board with three classes (2 + 2 + 3 = 7) cannot function with six members. If the co-founder/CTO is not a director, the charter's class assignments must be revised.

**Recommendation.** Determine whether Katerina Litvak/Litvanova is a director. If she is (per the charter), update the D&O questionnaire, the roadshow, and the lock-up schedule to reflect seven directors, and confirm the class assignments. If she is not, amend the charter's classified-board provisions before effectiveness. Ensure the S-1, charter, roadshow, and lock-up agreements are consistent.

### Finding 16 — Co-founder's legal name is inconsistent ("Litvak" vs. "Litvanova")

**Issue.** The co-founder/CTO's surname appears in two different forms across the document set, and the D&O questionnaire is internally inconsistent.

**Evidence.** "Katerina Litvak" (and "Katerina 'Kate' Litvak") appears in: the charter (Article IV, Section 4.3 Permitted Transferee); the bylaws (Article IV, Section 4.1); the underwriting term sheet (Section 1 and lock-up Schedule 1); the roadshow (Slides 5, 25, 30); and the D&O questionnaire certification block (Section 8.1). "Katerina Litvanova" (and "Katerina 'Kate' Litvanova") appears in: the CFO memo (Sections 2.2, 7.1); the D&O questionnaire body (Sections 1, 2.2, 4.1, 6); and the equity plan workbook (Option Grants and RSU Grants tabs). The D&O questionnaire Section 2.2 header says "Litvanova" while the same section's narrative refers to "Dr. Litvak," and the certification block says "LITVAK."

**Consequence.** The S-1 (cover page, signatures, Management section, principal stockholders, description of capital stock) and the charter must use the co-founder's correct legal name. An incorrect name in a filed registration statement or charter is a material error.

**Recommendation.** Confirm the co-founder's correct legal name from primary records (e.g., government-issued identification, the stock ledger, and the existing certificate of incorporation). Use that name consistently in the S-1, the charter, the bylaws, the underwriting agreement, the lock-up agreements, and all offering documents.

### Finding 17 — Graymont Ventures share count differs between the CFO memo and the D&O questionnaire

**Issue.** The number of shares attributed to Graymont Ventures differs by 4,934 shares across two documents.

**Evidence.** CFO memo Section 7.1: Graymont Ventures — 23,607,067 shares (22.4%). D&O questionnaire Section 3.1: "Total Graymont Shares: Approximately 23,602,133 shares (22.4%)."

**Consequence.** The S-1 principal stockholders table (Item 403) must state a precise share count. Even a small discrepancy indicates the figures are not reconciled to a single source of truth.

**Recommendation.** Reconcile Graymont's share count to the stock ledger (Oakvale/Computershare — see Finding 22) and use a single figure in the S-1.

### Finding 18 — Graymont and Ridgeline combined holdings exceed total preferred shares; series-level attribution does not reconcile

**Issue.** The reported beneficial ownership of the two largest stockholders (approximately 39.2 million shares combined) exceeds the total preferred shares outstanding (35,416,667), and the series-level attribution in the D&O questionnaire is internally impossible.

**Evidence.** D&O questionnaire: Graymont ~23,602,133 shares (Series A 8,000,000 + "a portion of Series B" + Series D) and Ridgeline ~15,594,267 shares (Series C 7,916,667 + Series D). Combined = 39,196,400, which exceeds total preferred (35,416,667) by 3,779,733. Further, if Ridgeline holds 7,677,600 Series D shares (15,594,267 − 7,916,667), then Graymont's Series D would be 2,322,400, leaving Graymont's Series B portion at 23,602,133 − 8,000,000 − 2,322,400 = 13,279,733 — which exceeds the entire Series B issuance of 9,500,000 shares. The attribution is therefore impossible unless Graymont also holds common stock (which the questionnaire does not mention).

**Consequence.** The S-1 principal stockholders table cannot be finalized until the share counts are reconciled to the cap table. The series-level breakdown in the S-1 (recent sales of unregistered securities and principal stockholders) must be accurate.

**Recommendation.** Obtain a complete cap table from the stock ledger reconciling each holder's common and preferred shares by series. Rebuild the principal stockholders table from that single source. Confirm whether Graymont (and/or Ridgeline) holds common stock in addition to preferred.

### Finding 19 — Ridgeline Growth Partners fund name is inconsistent ("Fund II" vs. "Fund V")

**Issue.** The fund entity holding Ridgeline's shares is identified differently in the CFO memo and the D&O questionnaire.

**Evidence.** CFO memo Section 7.1: "shares held by Ridgeline Growth Partners Fund II, L.P." D&O questionnaire Sections 3.4 and 4.1 footnote (2): "shares held by Ridgeline Growth Partners Fund V, LP."

**Consequence.** The S-1 principal stockholders footnotes and the lock-up agreements must identify the correct fund entity (the lock-up must bind the fund that actually holds the shares).

**Recommendation.** Confirm the correct fund name from the stock ledger and subscription agreements. Use it consistently in the S-1 footnotes and ensure the lock-up agreement is executed by the correct fund entity.

### Finding 20 — Founders' vested options do not appear to be reflected in the beneficial ownership table

**Issue.** The founders' large vested option positions (7.5 million options at $0.10 per share) do not appear to be included in the beneficial ownership figures.

**Evidence.** Equity plan workbook: OPT-2017-001 (Arun Ramaswamy, 4,000,000 options at $0.10, fully vested, 0 exercised, 4,000,000 outstanding) and OPT-2017-002 (Katerina Litvak, 3,500,000 options at $0.10, fully vested, 3,500,000 outstanding). CFO memo Section 7.1 beneficial ownership: Arun Ramaswamy 12,222,533 shares (11.6%) and Katerina Litvanova 9,693,733 shares (9.2%). The 12,222,533 figure exceeds the 12,200,000 common shares by only 22,533, and 9,693,733 exceeds 8,600,000 by 1,093,733 — neither reflects the 4,000,000 and 3,500,000 vested founder options. D&O questionnaire Section 2.1 lists Mr. Ramaswamy's options as "[●]" (blank).

**Consequence.** SEC rules require beneficial ownership to include shares issuable upon exercise of options exercisable within 60 days. Omitting 7.5 million deeply in-the-money vested options understates the founders' beneficial ownership and the "all directors and officers as a group" total, and affects the controlled-company and principal-stockholder percentages.

**Recommendation.** Recompute the founders' beneficial ownership to include all vested/exercisable options (4,000,000 and 3,500,000 at $0.10, plus any other vested awards and RSUs vesting within 60 days). Update the principal stockholders table and the "all directors and officers as a group" total.

### Finding 21 — Aldersgate Capital (Series B participant) current ownership is unconfirmed; may be a 5%+ holder

**Issue.** A Series B participant's current ownership is not reported in any beneficial ownership disclosure, and the roadshow refers to it by a different name.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 10 and D&O questionnaire Section 4.2 identify "Aldersgate Capital" as a Series B (April 2020) participant. D&O questionnaire Open Item 3: Aldersgate "does not appear in any current beneficial ownership disclosure … The S-1 principal stockholders table (Item 403) cannot be finalized until this matter is resolved." Roadshow Slide 29 notes refer to "Crestview Capital" as the Series B participant — a different name.

**Consequence.** If Aldersgate Capital (or "Crestview Capital") currently holds 5% or more of any class of securities, it must be included in the S-1 principal stockholders table (Item 403) and in the lock-up party list. The name discrepancy ("Aldersgate" vs. "Crestview") must be resolved. The principal stockholders table cannot be finalized without this confirmation.

**Recommendation.** Confirm from the stock ledger whether Aldersgate Capital (or Crestview Capital) currently holds Crestline shares, and in what amount. If it holds 5%+, obtain a completed D&O questionnaire, add it to the principal stockholders table, and include it in the lock-up agreements. Reconcile the name.

### Finding 22 — Transfer agent is identified inconsistently ("Computershare" vs. "Oakvale")

**Issue.** The bylaws identify a different transfer agent than every other document.

**Evidence.** Bylaws Article V, Section 5.1: "The transfer agent and registrar for the Corporation's stock shall be Computershare Transfer Co." CFO memo, underwriting term sheet (Section 1 and lock-up Section 6), and D&O questionnaire Open Item 3: "Oakvale Transfer Transfer Co." (the name also contains an apparent duplication, "Transfer Transfer").

**Consequence.** The S-1 and the bylaws must identify the correct transfer agent. The apparent typo in "Oakvale Transfer Transfer Co." should be corrected.

**Recommendation.** Confirm the engaged transfer agent. Update the bylaws (if Oakvale is correct) or the other documents (if Computershare is correct) so they agree. Correct the "Transfer Transfer" duplication.

## IV. EQUITY AWARDS AND 409A VALUATION

### Finding 23 — 409A valuation history conflicts between the audited financial statements and the equity plan records

**Issue.** The 409A common-stock valuation history in the audited financial statements differs materially from the history in the equity plan workbook, with only two dates in common.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 12(e) 409A valuations: June 30, 2022 ($7.80); September 30, 2022 ($9.50); March 31, 2023 ($14.20); September 30, 2023 ($16.50); March 31, 2024 ($18.20); September 30, 2024 ($19.60). Equity plan workbook "409A Valuations" tab: March 14, 2017 ($0.10); September 30, 2018 ($0.85); March 31, 2020 ($2.40); September 30, 2020 ($3.10); December 31, 2021 ($7.20); September 30, 2022 ($9.50); March 31, 2023 ($14.20); December 31, 2023 ($16.80); December 31, 2024 preliminary ($18.50). Only September 30, 2022 ($9.50) and March 31, 2023 ($14.20) match. The most recent audited valuation is $19.60 (September 30, 2024); the equity plan's most recent is a preliminary $18.50 (December 31, 2024) — a decline that is inconsistent with the approaching IPO.

**Consequence.** The S-1's stock-based compensation disclosure and the 409A valuation history must be consistent with the audited financial statements. The equity plan workbook's valuation history (which was used to set option exercise prices) conflicts with the audited disclosure. The equity plan workbook also states the December 31, 2024 valuation "will be referenced in the S-1 registration statement for stock-based compensation disclosure purposes," conflicting with the audited September 30, 2024 valuation of $19.60.

**Recommendation.** Reconcile the 409A valuation history to a single, authoritative set of valuation reports (from Meridian Valuation Partners LLC). The S-1 should present the valuation history consistent with the audited financial statements. Confirm which valuation supports the most recent SBC expense and any pre-IPO grants.

### Finding 24 — "Cheap stock" / Section 409A risk on Q1 2023 option grants

**Issue.** A large option grant was priced using a 409A valuation that a subsequent valuation increased by approximately 49.5%, raising a cheap-stock and 409A compliance concern.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 12(e) and equity plan workbook: in Q1 2023, the Company granted 2,400,000 options at $9.50 per share based on the September 30, 2022 409A valuation ($9.50). A subsequent 409A valuation dated March 31, 2023 determined fair value to be $14.20 per share (a 49.5% increase). The auditor identified "Valuation of Common Stock Underlying Stock-Based Compensation Awards" as a critical audit matter.

**Consequence.** If the $9.50 exercise price was below the fair value of the common stock at the grant date, the Company may have a "cheap stock" accounting issue (additional SBC expense) and a Section 409A compliance exposure for the option holders. The SEC staff commonly comments on option grants priced below a contemporaneous or subsequent fair value.

**Recommendation.** Obtain a 409A compliance analysis for the Q1 2023 grants. Confirm whether the September 30, 2022 valuation remained the "most recent available" valuation at each grant date and whether the 12-month safe harbor applies. Disclose the matter and any additional SBC expense in the S-1 if warranted.

### Finding 25 — Several option grants were priced using valuations dated after the grant date (retrospective pricing)

**Issue.** The equity plan workbook indicates that certain option grants were priced using 409A valuations that were completed after the grant date.

**Evidence.** Equity plan workbook: OPT-2021-002 (granted June 15, 2021) references the December 31, 2021 valuation ($7.20), with a note that the "409A valuation as of 12/31/2021 completed retrospectively and confirmed FMV for grant date pricing." OPT-2022-003 (granted June 1, 2022) references the September 30, 2022 valuation ($9.50), with a note that the "valuation completed 11/14/2022; grants priced retrospectively." A valuation dated after a grant cannot, by itself, establish the fair value at the grant date for 409A purposes.

**Consequence.** Pricing a grant with a valuation that did not exist at the grant date is a 409A compliance concern and may indicate that the exercise prices were not supported by a contemporaneous fair value determination, creating cheap-stock accounting and tax exposure.

**Recommendation.** Review each grant priced with a retrospective valuation. For any grant where the exercise price may have been below fair value at the grant date, assess the SBC and 409A consequences and disclose as appropriate. Ensure all future grants are priced using a valuation dated on or before the grant date.

### Finding 26 — Equity plan workbook option activity does not reconcile to the audited option rollforward

**Issue.** The option grants, exercises, and forfeitures in the equity plan workbook do not reconcile to the audited option rollforward in Note 12(b), and the workbook's totals do not foot.

**Evidence.** Audited Note 12(b) rollforward (which foots): FY2023 — begin 12,500,000 + granted 2,700,000 − exercised 1,100,000 − forfeited 300,000 = end 13,800,000; FY2024 — begin 13,800,000 + granted 2,100,000 − exercised 800,000 − forfeited 350,000 = end 14,750,000. Equity plan workbook Option Grants tab: 2023 grants sum to 4,140,000 (vs. 2,700,000 audited); 2024 grants sum to 2,940,000 (vs. 2,100,000 audited); total "Shares Exercised" 60,000 (vs. 1,900,000 exercised over the two audited years); total "Shares Cancelled/Forfeited" 1,100,000 (vs. 650,000 over the two audited years). The workbook's "TOTALS" row (18,910,000 shares granted) does not equal the sum of the individual grant rows (21,490,000), and the granted/exercised/forfeited/outstanding figures cannot produce a valid rollforward (they imply a negative beginning balance).

**Consequence.** The equity plan records that feed the S-1's equity award tables (Note 12, the Recent Sales of Unregistered Securities section, and the executive compensation tables) do not tie to the audited financial statements. The ending outstanding (14,750,000) matches, but the activity does not, indicating the workbook is not a reliable source for the S-1.

**Recommendation.** Rebuild the equity award tables in the S-1 from the audited rollforward and the stock ledger/option register, not from the equity plan workbook. Reconcile the workbook to the audited activity and correct the workbook's internal totals.

### Finding 27 — RSU vesting is inconsistent (double-trigger liquidity condition vs. audited vesting)

**Issue.** The equity plan workbook states that all RSUs are subject to an IPO liquidity-event condition (double-trigger) and that none have vested, but the audited financial statements show RSUs vesting in FY2023 and FY2024.

**Evidence.** Equity plan workbook RSU Grants tab: every RSU grant states "settlement subject to IPO liquidity event condition (double-trigger: time-based vesting + liquidity event)" and "Shares Vested as of 12/31/2024 = 0" with status "Outstanding — Unvested; liquidity condition not yet met." Audited equity rollforward (Section 4) and Note 12(c): "Vesting of restricted stock units" 200,000 in FY2023 and 700,000 in FY2024. The cash flow statement shows "Taxes paid related to net share settlement of RSUs" $2.536 million in FY2024, indicating RSU settlement occurred.

**Consequence.** If the RSUs are truly double-trigger (requiring an IPO liquidity event), no vesting or tax withholding should have occurred before the IPO, and the audited vesting/settlement would be incorrect. If the RSUs are single-trigger (time-based only), the equity plan workbook's double-trigger description is wrong. This affects the SBC expense, the share count, and the S-1's description of the equity awards.

**Recommendation.** Determine the actual vesting terms of the RSUs from the award agreements. Reconcile the vesting/settlement in the audited financial statements with the award terms. Correct either the audited financial statements or the equity plan workbook so they agree, and describe the vesting terms accurately in the S-1.

### Finding 28 — RSU grant totals do not reconcile

**Issue.** The RSUs granted in FY2024 per the equity plan workbook do not match the audited RSU rollforward.

**Evidence.** Equity plan workbook RSU Grants tab: FY2024 RSU grants sum to 2,450,000. Audited Note 12(c): FY2024 RSUs granted 1,400,000. (The audited rollforward foots: begin 2,800,000 + granted 1,400,000 − vested 700,000 − forfeited 300,000 = end 3,200,000.) The workbook's ending outstanding (3,200,000) matches the audited ending, but the grant activity does not. The workbook also has a small internal discrepancy (3,400,000 granted − 180,000 forfeited − 0 settled = 3,220,000, but outstanding is stated as 3,200,000).

**Recommendation.** Rebuild the RSU table in the S-1 from the audited rollforward. Reconcile the equity plan workbook to the audited activity.

### Finding 29 — Director names in the equity plan workbook are wrong

**Issue.** The equity plan workbook lists director RSU grants under names that do not match the actual directors.

**Evidence.** Equity plan workbook RSU Grants tab (June 15, 2024 director grants): "Anand Venkatesh," "Claudia Moreno," "David Park," "Soo-Yeon Kwon," and "Emmanuel Obi" — each labeled "Non-Employee Director." The actual non-employee directors are Priya Venkatesh, Thomas J. Moreno, Dr. Helen Park, James Kwon, and Adaora Obi. The first names do not match.

**Consequence.** The director compensation table in the S-1 (Item 402) and the equity award tables would be wrong if sourced from the workbook. This is a data-integrity failure in the equity plan records.

**Recommendation.** Correct the director names in the equity plan records. Source the director compensation and equity award disclosures in the S-1 from corrected records. Confirm that the 15,000 RSU director grants (5 × 15,000) were actually made to the correct directors.

### Finding 30 — Officer titles/roles in the equity plan workbook are wrong

**Issue.** The equity plan workbook assigns incorrect titles to officers and invents a "General Counsel" who is not the Company's General Counsel.

**Evidence.** Equity plan workbook: OPT-2018-002 and OPT-2024-005 and RSU-2024-004 list "Sandra Chen" as "VP Sales (now CRO)" — but Sandra Y. Chen is the General Counsel and Corporate Secretary (per the bylaws, CFO memo, D&O questionnaire, and roadshow). OPT-2022-002 and RSU-2024-005 list "Thomas Brennan" as "General Counsel" — but the Company's General Counsel is Sandra Chen; "Thomas Brennan" does not appear as an officer in any other document. "David Park" is listed both as "VP Product" (OPT-2021-002) and as a "Non-Employee Director" (RSU-2024-011), which is contradictory.

**Consequence.** The S-1's executive compensation and equity award disclosures would be incorrect if sourced from the workbook. The existence of an unreported "General Counsel" (Thomas Brennan) and a "CRO" (Sandra Chen) conflicts with the Management section.

**Recommendation.** Correct the officer titles in the equity plan records. Confirm whether Thomas Brennan is or was an employee and whether any "CRO" position exists. Ensure the S-1 Management section and compensation tables reflect the correct officers and titles.

### Finding 31 — The 2017 Stock Option Plan is not disclosed in the audited financial statements

**Issue.** The equity plan workbook describes a predecessor 2017 Stock Option Plan that was rolled into the 2024 Plan, but the audited financial statements do not mention the 2017 Plan.

**Evidence.** Equity plan workbook Plan Overview tab: the 2024 Equity Incentive Plan is the "Successor to 2017 Stock Option Plan; all outstanding awards under 2017 Plan rolled into 2024 Plan." Audited Note 12(a) describes only the "2024 Equity Incentive Plan" and does not mention the 2017 Plan or the roll-over. Option grants from 2017 through 2023 (per the workbook) were made under the 2017 Plan.

**Consequence.** The S-1 should describe the plan history (2017 Plan → 2024 Plan) and the roll-over of outstanding awards, and should file both plan documents as exhibits if awards remain outstanding under the 2017 Plan terms.

**Recommendation.** Add a description of the 2017 Stock Option Plan and the roll-over to the S-1's equity plan disclosure. Confirm whether the 2017 Plan document should be filed as an exhibit.
